# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Need a movie that will actually scare me.

**Score:** 79
**Body:** I was 11 years old when the 1985 movie "House" was released on video. That movie scared the crap out of me & started a lifelong love of horror movies.  I'm now fifty years old and have yet to find a movie that scares me in the same way. Any suggestions?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't think anything is ever going to scare you the way you got scared as a kid. Only real life stuff will do that to a 50-year old person. 

Having said that, the 2 *IT* movies from 2017 and 2019 had the kind of imagery that would have made them the scariest movies I'd ever seen, if I saw them when I was 11.
  - The most scared I've ever been in a theater was the movie Annabelle, but that was only one particular scene and in a theater atmosphere.  Literally, I had goosebumps form on me.  

I will say that Barbarian was scary (to me) in the middle part before it jumped the shark, even watching it from home, and the ending sequence of Hereditary was really messed up when I saw that in theaters.
2. Bring Her Back
  - More wincing and uncomfortable than scary/terror for me. Really well done though, I also like Talk to Me by the same directors.
3. It's not the movies anymore its the age-you don't scare that easy anymore-at 50 I would suggest watching the news if you really want to experience horror.
4. Insidious
5. #Blair Witch Project

Then go for a walk in the deep woods by yourself at night. I guarantee that'll evoke the same reaction.

It's not always about the experience whilst watching the movie; it's how it lingers with you afterwards.
6. sinister?
7. It Follows scared me as an adult the first time I saw it.
8. The Empty Man (2020)

## 2. Suggest Movie With Unexpected Endings Like Se7en

**Score:** 29
**Body:** I’m looking for some really intense and mind-bending movies similar to Seven that have shocking, unexpected endings. You know, the kind of films that leave you completely speechless, sitting in silence, just trying to process what actually happened. I want those movies where the story builds up in such a gripping way that the final twist turns everything upside down and makes you rethink the entire film. Something dark, psychological, and unpredictable, where the ending feels like a punch in the gut and stays with you long after the credits roll. Basically, movies that leave you shocked and asking yourself, “Wait, what just happened?” Can you suggest some films that fit this description, with suspense and unforgettable endings?


### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The twist in the movie r/MovieSuggestions - where the same question gets asked twice a week phrased slightly differently, and it turns out the sub is trapped in a time loop, doomed to answer the same question with the exact same answers over and over for all eternity.
2. Its quite obvious but... the Sixth Sense and Usual Suspects.
3. Prestige

Incendies

Oldboy
  - I’ve been putting off Incendies even though I love DV’s movies.  I guess it’s worth watching?
4. The Sixth Sense
Fight Club
Vanilla Sky
Memento
The Machinist
Identity
The Usual Suspects
The Others
The Game
The Village
5. Speak No evil ( Danish original) is excellent 

American version has a different ending .

The Gift ( Rebecca Hall) 

The Invisible Guest ( Spanish)
  - The Gift!
6. Weapons

Fight Club
7. Gone baby gone
8. Sixth Sense and Shutter Island

## 3. Best 80s comedies for someone whose favorite movies are R Rated comedies from the 2000s?

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Favorite comedy movies from the era are Road Trip, Wedding Crashers, The Hangover, Role Models, Superbad, Bad Santa, American Pie 2 (#1 came out in 99), Dude Where's My Car 

Favorite 80s comedy so far: Fast Times At Ridgemont High 

Back To School was a lot of fun, too, but Fast Times was a much better movie imo 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Blues Brothers (1980)

Trading Places (1983)
2. Porky's the first two
3. Bachelor Party. It’s got tom hanks and tawny kitaen in it. So raunchy, so quotable.
4. Heathers
5. This is spinal tap
6. Ferris Bueller's Day Off
7. Caddy Shack

Easy Money 

Fletch

Beverly Hills Cop

Armed And Dangerous 

The Burbs 

Ghost Busters 

The Great Outdoors 

1941

Big Trouble in Little China 

Spaceballs

History of the World: part 1
8. Eating Raoul 
9. Revenge of the Nerds
10. Yes, you've got my taste. 

National lampoon vacation, Airplane, three Amigos, Monty Python, Blues Brothers. 

Btw I just watched Johnny Dangerously and it's hilarious. The plot doesn't make sense but my goodness, it made me laugh. So underrated and no one knows about this one

## 4. I need some fun murder mystery for this dreary day

**Score:** 21
**Body:** It's Sunday folks. I am dreading work tomorrow. Today is so boring. It's really cloudy and dreary where I am. I am in the mood for some fun murder mystery that doesn't take itself completely seriously. It's also autumn and Halloween is around the corner so something keeping with the theme would be nice to start getting into it 

What do you have for me?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Clue!
  - Yes!
  - Clue it is :)
2. Murder by Death :)
3. A Shot in the Dark (1964)…part of the Pink Panther franchise. Perhaps you might want to consider the other movies in this franchise.
4. Knives Out

Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery

Wake Up Dead Man

Murder On The Orient Express

Death On The Nile

A Haunting In Venice
5. Fargo
6. Deathtrap
7. Clue.
8. An Inspector Calls (1954)

## 5. Movies that deals with conspiracy theories.

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Movies where seeking out truth behind a conspiracy theories.

Any genre is welcome as long as it fits the description.

I don't want movies that merely touch a conspiracy and leave it, like eddington for example. 

I want the conspiracy they deal with as the main plot

It would be even better if the protagonist is young and curious.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Conspiracy Theory (1997)
2. Oliver Stone’s JFK. That movie is EXACTLY what you’re craving.
3. Enemy of the State (1998)
4. Parallax View, with Warren Beatty, is perfect.  Steeped in the paranoia of the post Watergate era.
5. The Manchurian Candidate. The remake with Denzel Washington is awesome.
  - The original still holds up.
6. Three Days of the Condor
7. The Conspiracy (2012) its a found footage movie but do not be fooled. It is pretty entertaining
8. Enemy of The State
9. They Live is so good.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What ever happened to Evan Rachel wood?

**Score:** 1673

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. She played Madonna in the Weird Al movie (2022), she was hilarious in that.
  - That movie was so much better than it had any right being. She and Daniel Radcliffe were incredible in it.
  - Madonna is still at large and needs to answer for her crimes
2. She was staring in West World up until recently. Not sure if she’s done anything noteworthy since, but that show was huge for a while.
3. She was a vampire in True Blood.
  - Queen Sophie Anne.  Wanna play Yahtzee?
  - And What We Do In the Shadows
    - All of the celebs that revisited their prior vampire roles from other movies, to form The Vampire Council in “What We Do In The Shadows”, all of whom used their real names, in particular, Tilda, Evan, Paul, and Danny, was pure cameo gold.
4. She was in Frozen 2!
5. She's also been doing some theater acting. She was in New York playing Audrey in Little Shop of Horrors last year and they sold out almost every night.

## 2. what's that movie you regret watching?

**Score:** 1321

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Human Centipede..
  - Should I eat the Cuttlefish and Asparagus or the vanilla paste?
    - Vanilla paste!! Vanilla Paste!!!!
      - I BERIEVE IN YOU!!!!
2. A Serbian Film
  - I read the description, actually watching it seems like an act of self-harm.
    - It is.. it is
  - I read an article from a guy who watched it twice! The second time was to asses the movie from a technical point of view. His conclusion was that it wasn’t a good movie, technically speaking.

I will believe his word.
3. Cannibal Holocaust

Especially consdering I thought the BBFC cut all thr animal killings out not just the Coatimundi
4. Red Notice on Netflix.

## 3. What’s a “non biopic” biopic. A film clearly based on a real person but also fictionalised and not using specific names.

**Score:** 63
**Body:** Citizen Kane clearly being Rudolph William Hearst. What are some other examples of this? 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Walk Hard: The Dewey Cox Story
  - In reality, it was Johnny Cash's sister that got halved
2. Johnny Fontane in the Godfather is supposed to be Sinatra.
3. Primary Colors was more or less based on Bill Clinton's run for president.
4. *Casino* is based on Frank Rosenthal, with the name changed to Sam Rothstein. Joe Pesci’s character Nicky is based on mobster Anthony Spilotro, and Sharon Stone’s character Ginger is based on Rosenthal’s wife Geri.
5. 8 mile is clearly loosely based on Eminem’s life but he’s called B Rabbit in it.

Mollys Game had a character based on Tobey Maguire but isn’t named so
  - I don’t think they even tried to pretend with that one. I remember seeing it and everyone just thought “The film where Eminem plays Eminem”.
6. The Devil Wears Prada...
7. The Master, but it’s only very, very loosely based on L. Ron Hubbard.
8. **The Brutalist**, based on architects Marcel Breuer and Ernö Goldfinger.

## 4. Who is your favorite antihero from TV shows?

**Score:** 35
**Body:** Saul Goodman from Better Call Saul.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. * Omar Little
* Saul Goodman
* The Hound
2. Dexter from the show “Dexter”
3. You hit the nail on the head OP.  SG was a bastard; but seeing his transformation from JM was a great piece of work!
4. Boyd Crowder in Justified
5. Saul is a great one.
6. Tony Soprano
7. Detective McNulty from the Wire. He’s a spectacular trainwreck of a human being; A fucking loser with a divorced wife and kids who barely see him, a functional alcoholic who’s more than willing to step on and fuck over whoever he has to in order to finish his case and get what he wants. He’s insubordinate, sleeps around, drinks constantly. 

But he’s also one of the best detectives in the entire Baltimore PD and a good beat cop when he puts his mind to it with a somewhat twisted but nonetheless strict sense of right and wrong. The modern American hero.
8. Al Swearengen - Deadwood

The ULTIMATE anti-hero
9. Walter White (Breaking Bad) or Marty Byrde (Ozark)
10. Will from Hannibal. Great show, but that boy ain't right!

## 5. What's your ultimate "bad day" comfort movie?

**Score:** 30
**Body:** You know the one. You've had a terrible, no-good, rotten day and you just need to shut your brain off and feel better.

What's the movie you automatically put on that never fails you? Not necessarily the "best" movie, but the one that's like a warm blanket. For a lot of people it's something like *The Princess Bride* or *Step Brothers*. What's yours?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy

Really, I am not joking, it’s gotten me through some bad days. I think I find it calming to concentrate on the intricacies of the story and the characters, rather than my own. Whereas cheerful movies just seem to point out my inability to maintain my own happiness.
2. Office Space
3. Grand Budapest Hotel
4. The Big Lebowski
5. Kung-Fu Panda or LOTR. Depends on the bad day/mood.
6. Little miss sunshine
7. This may seem different from everyone else's responses but I turn to thrillers or suspense and horror when I had a bad day.  Sometimes the rom-coms make me feel worse because I am a single person.  I watch some from the 80's and some older classics

Internal Affairs with Andy Garcia and Richard Gere  
Bad Influence- James Spader/ Rob Lowe

Malice- Nicole Kidman/Alec Baldwin/Bill Pullman

Marnie- Alfred Hitchcock 

Rosemary's Baby- Mia Farrow

Serpico, Dog Day Afternoon- Al Pacino

The Conversation- Gene Hackman

The Other (1975).

The Others- (2001)

Ghost Story (1980)
8. Dumb and Dumber. The scene with the diarrhea gushing out of his backside always has me shitting myself lol. Plus that woman in it is hot af.
9. Blade Runner
10. The Emperer’s new grove and Shrek

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. After Hours

**Score:** 21
**Body:** So, I recently revisited After Hours, and for whatever reason (this is my third viewing), it became obvious that this is an absurdist classic, mimicking the typical trajectory of absurdist fiction in which a protagonist comes into contact with the chaotic nature of the universe and is eventually pushed to the point of madness due to its illogical nature.

Anyway, I wanted to talk about the intertextual metaphor of Munch’s painting ‘The Scream’ through the paper-mache art. I don’t if this is referenced much in absurdist philosophy, but I don’t think it would be far-fetch to connect this painting, which depicts a suffering howl against the universe, to the realm of absurdism (especially the scream against universe that occurs when one comes into to contact with the inherent unfairness of an absurdist world). 

If one we’re to view ‘The Scream’ in this manner, that it depicts a howl against an inherently unjust and chaotic universe, then does Paul throughout After Hours slowly morph into Munch’s vision? For no particular reason, Paul is punished in an inherently absurd fashion. In addition, he does become a paper-mache in the end that is an obvious nod to the Munch-esque paper-mache at the beginning. Also, in a similar fashion to the painting, Paul howls at the sky due to the inherent indifference of the universe to his own suffering. 

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I am now remembering the 'purgatory' theory lol. I am also of the absurd punishment mindset. Same way he gets illogically distrustful of the people he encounters in SoHo and starts thinking the worst of them due to his class bias, they in turn get illogically distrustful of him and think the worst of him, in turn feeding each other's bias.
2. I loved the straight lift of Kafka's parable "before the law" from The Trial, put into the mouth of a nightclub bouncer at Club Berlin.

After Hours feels like the zenith of yuppie nightmare: the city turns against those with power and privilege and their howl at the dissonance of having no route back to safety.  

“Here no one else can gain entry, since this entrance was assigned only to you. I’m going now to close it."

## 2. What sets Kubrick’s films apart from others?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Kubrick, or Scorsese, or whatever great director - yes their films are excellent, but what about them is so great it sets them far from any other directors? Maybe it’s because I’m young, or maybe don’t have enough media literacy, but I never really understood how or why they are considered to be the greatest of all time. To be clear, I did understand the movies (to a basic level, at least), and liked them, I just never understood why they are considered some of the best.

For context the only Kubrick films ive seen are The Shining, Eyes Wide Shut, and Full Metal Jacket (I loved all of them, FMJ the most. I plan on finishing his whole filmography soon). 

Could someone try help me understand, maybe with a few specific examples as to why? (To any die-hard Kubrick fans, please be nice in the replies)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t know if anyone is going to say anything that could really help you understand. You kind of just have to “get it”, but there’s nothing wrong with not understanding right away.

When I first started watching films, I didn’t really get why the greats were considered so great. But now, after watching thousands more films, it’s just extremely obvious to me whenever I watch a Kubrick or Hitchcock or Bergman just how brilliant they were.

It’s about the perfection of execution, the depth of ideas, the unmistakable identity they infuse into their style, the attention to detail in every aspect from the acting to set design and beyond, and the cohesion of everything to create a rich and powerful experience. Watching their films feels like having a conversation with them, they know how to use their artform to communicate to their audience in a way that other directors just don’t. And they have a way of getting deep under the skin, into the subconscious in a way you might not immediately realize.
2. Kubrick is known for being meticulous in detail. If he did a period piece, he would literally look at the Farmer’s Almanac for weather conditions at that time, and film during similar weather. For Barry Lyndon, he repurposed a NASA lense so that his cinematographer could film in complete natural light. Truly one of the most beautiful films ever put to picture, even if the subject of said film bores you to tears (it really doesn’t, hopefully). His passion for filmmaking, in my opinion, is unmatched save for Kurosawa. He was extremely passionate, often to much detriment, in making films.
3. The considered framing. The measured tempo. The choice of narratives where there's no intent for the audience to identify with the ostensible protagonists. Any who appear charismatic have that drained away. The characters in Kubrick films are fallible and often very flawed humans, acting without much sense of will or control, and Kubrick's eye examines them with a cold eye. Like insects under glass.

There are many who despise Kubrick films, as the films don't exhibit much humanity towards their characters. But for those of us who have been touched by world weariness or misanthropy, its refreshing.
4. Quality: Kubrick films don't look or feel like other films. There is a unique vision that persisted from Paths of Glory all the way to Eyes Wide Shut. Everything from the caustic tone of his films to the technical innovations he made just set him apart from other directors. It's hard to explain why something is good, and the only way to do it is to compare his films to those of his contemporaries and notice the difference in quality.

Longevity: He started his career making films alongside Hitchcock and Billy Wilder, and ended it alongside Fincher and Tarantino. He maintained relevance and influence for over 40 years. Other than Scorsese, no one else has managed to do that.

Consistency: Besides his first two short films, Kubrick never really made a bad film. You could argue about how good/great each film was, but the overall quality of his filmography is uncontested. This sense of completeness further cemented his place in the canon.
5. As for Kubrick: He contributed masterpieces to several genres and nearly all of them became reference points for that genre. His visual style (wide shots, strict geometry, character cameras) was revolutionary and his use of music (see 2001 or Clockwork orange) unparalleled. Many if his films (especially Barry Lyndon and Clockwork Orange) look vivid because of the detailed attention to costume design and colour
  - Ir's absolutely not true that Scorsese "mostly does gangster films".  Have you watched his movies?
    - Not saying he exclusively does them but many of his films are following crime stories in different settings. 
This wasnt meant to be a generalization but a contrast to Kubrick whose films vary more strongly in terms of setting

Took that line out bc discussing that is obviously not what OP was interested in 
6. He’s in that very rare class of artist whose work seems qualitatively different from just about any other person working in the medium. I feel the same about Tolstoy - reading his novels isn’t like reading anything else. And it’s hard to put into words why such artists are so great, almost like we don’t have the language because it’s so transcendent and rare.

That Kubrick achieved this within the medium of film, which is so notoriously hard because of the necessary compromise with producers looking at the bottom line, and in such an insanely poor environment to create great art (his own image of “like writing War and Peace in a bumper car” is very apt) is all the more extraordinary.

---
